sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Urinary tract infection ranges from uncomplicated cystitis to upper tract infection with sepsis risk. Prehospital assessment should identify systemic illness, pregnancy, urinary obstruction, or complicated infection, while providing symptom relief and appropriate escalation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Lower UTI:**
- Dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic discomfort
- Cloudy or malodorous urine
- Occasional haematuria

**Upper UTI or complicated infection:**
- Fever, rigors, flank pain, nausea, or vomiting
- Hypotension, tachycardia, confusion, or sepsis features

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Men, pregnant patients, catheterised patients, immunocompromised patients, and those with systemic features need a lower threshold for escalation
- Consider STI, pyelonephritis, renal colic, urinary retention, abdominal pathology, or sepsis in the differential diagnosis
- Urinary alkalinisers can reduce the activity of nitrofurantoin and should not be started casually if antimicrobial treatment is being considered
- New confusion alone should not be assumed to represent UTI without broader assessment for other causes

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey and full vital sign survey
- Urinalysis may help support the assessment where available but should not replace clinical judgement
- Assess for flank pain, fever, vomiting, pregnancy, urinary retention, catheter issues, or systemic illness
- Encourage oral fluids if tolerated
- Consider simple analgesia or antipyretics for discomfort or fever
- Safety-net clearly for worsening pain, fever, vomiting, confusion, or reduced urine output

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider antiemetic treatment if nausea or vomiting is significant
- Consider IV access and fluids for dehydration, sepsis risk, or inability to tolerate oral intake
- Antibiotics should be doctor-directed or protocol-led, especially in pregnancy, male patients, systemic illness, or upper UTI
- Manage septic presentations according to the sepsis pathway
- Escalate early for obstruction concern, pyelonephritis, pregnancy, catheter complications, or systemic deterioration

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Lack of improvement within 48 to 72 hours, or recurrent symptoms, warrants reassessment
- New confusion in older adults should not be attributed to UTI without a broader assessment
